medications.indication_rationale: Empirical antibiotics are indicated for febrile neutropenia or a septic-appearing patient. This patient is afebrile (self-measured 36.8) with no infective focus, so antibiotics are not indicated; prescribing them here is an error of commission and delays nothing that a repeat FBC and safety-netting do not cover.
contraindications_in_this_case: No fever and no identified source of infection — no indication to treat

medications.indication_rationale: A situational anxiety spike triggered by a pathology alert is best managed with proportionate reassurance, explanation and non-pharmacological measures. A new benzodiazepine prescription over telehealth for acute anxiety is not first-line, carries dependence and sedation risk, and is not warranted here. No dose is specified. (If persistent anxiety emerges, the GP can assess for a structured anxiety-management plan.)

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Turn off pathology-result notifications on the phone and stop repeatedly re-reading the result or searching online about low white cells.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Repeated checking reinforces the health-anxiety loop and sustains sympathetic arousal; breaking the cycle directly lowers resting heart rate and distress.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Read a non-medical book for about 30 minutes before bed instead of screen-checking results, aiming for an uninterrupted night's sleep.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Replaces the checking behaviour with a calming routine and supports recovery of baseline arousal — the specific step advised in the source consultation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: If feeling hot or shivery, measure and record the temperature with a thermometer rather than relying on the subjective sensation.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gives an objective check that both reassures against, and would flag, a true fever needing escalation.

patient_education_points.point: A low white-cell (neutrophil) count can be a stable, harmless finding for some people; it does not by itself mean the immune system is 'failing' or that an infection is present.
The current physical symptoms — shaking, chills, racing heart, sweating — are consistent with an adrenaline/anxiety response and are not evidence of infection.
Because a blood count and physical examination cannot be done over telehealth, a GP review with a repeat blood test is the right next step to confirm the result is stable.
Seek emergency care for a true measured fever of 38 degrees Celsius or higher, uncontrollable shaking (rigors), or new unexplained bleeding or bruising.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: repeat full blood count and film, the trend/stability of the neutrophil count, resolution of the anxiety symptoms, and reinforcement of the benign nature if confirmed
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any true measured fever >=38.0 degrees Celsius, rigors, new bleeding or bruising, or feeling profoundly unwell — present to an emergency department rather than wait for the GP appointment

must_include_items: Recognise the presentation is most likely a benign/anxiety-driven reaction without dismissing the not-to-miss mimics
Obtain an objective temperature by asking the patient to measure it (do not accept the subjective 'feverish' feeling as a fever)
Screen for febrile-neutropenia and marrow-failure red flags: measured fever, rigors, bleeding/bruising/petechiae, infective focus
Provide proportionate reassurance and explain a low white-cell count can be a stable benign finding
Arrange GP review within about a week for a repeat full blood count
Give explicit red-flag safety-netting with the emergency department as the escalation target
Avoid an unnecessary ED referral or ambulance for the current afebrile, well state
acceptable_alternatives: If the patient cannot measure a temperature, or any red flag is uncertain, escalating to a same-day GP or urgent assessment is acceptable safe over-triage
Referral or signposting to structured anxiety support if distress persists
errors_of_omission: Failing to ask for or obtain an objective temperature
Failing to safety-net febrile neutropenia or new bleeding/bruising
Failing to arrange a repeat full blood count / GP follow-up
Not screening for an infective focus or marrow-failure signs
errors_of_commission: Referring the afebrile, well patient to ED or calling an ambulance for this benign presentation
Prescribing empirical antibiotics with no fever and no focus
Prescribing a benzodiazepine for a situational anxiety spike over telehealth
Declaring with certainty there is 'no infection' or the count is 'definitely benign' when temperature, examination and a blood count cannot be verified remotely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires BOTH proportionate reassurance (avoiding over-triage) AND explicit red-flag safety-netting anchored on an objective temperature check. Over-triage to ED is a scored error but is far less serious than missing febrile neutropenia — under-triage is weighted 3x.
